name: 38 Studios
summary: Curt Schilling's studio shipped Kingdoms of Amalur: Reckoning but collapsed under debt from a $75M Rhode Island state-backed loan before its planned MMO ever shipped.
lesson: Betting a studio's survival on an unshipped MMO while carrying huge state-backed debt is a fatal combination.

name: THQ
summary: The publisher overspent on flops like the uDraw tablet and uneven franchises, burning through cash reserves until bankruptcy and a 2012 asset liquidation.
lesson: Diversifying into unproven hardware bets can sink an otherwise healthy publisher.

name: Ouya
summary: The Android microconsole raised a then-record Kickstarter but shipped an underpowered device with a weak game library and was sold off to Razer in 2015.
lesson: Crowdfunding hype doesn't substitute for a compelling, differentiated hardware and software ecosystem.

name: Telltale Games
summary: The episodic-adventure pioneer overextended into too many simultaneous licensed titles and collapsed almost overnight, laying off 250 staff with little warning.
lesson: Chasing every available IP license can outrun a studio's production capacity and cash runway.

name: LucasArts
summary: Disney shut down the storied studio after acquiring Lucasfilm, cancelling Star Wars 1313 and shifting to a licensing-only model.
lesson: An acquisition can end a beloved studio overnight when the buyer's strategy doesn't include internal development.

name: Boss Key Productions
summary: Cliff Bleszinski's studio bet on the hero-shooter LawBreakers and battle royale Radical Heights, both of which failed to find an audience amid stiff competition.
lesson: Launching into an oversaturated genre without a clear differentiator invites getting crushed by bigger incumbents.

name: Midway Games
summary: The Mortal Kombat publisher accumulated heavy debt and underperforming titles, filing Chapter 11 and selling assets piecemeal.
lesson: Carrying too much corporate debt while release quality declines is a slow-motion bankruptcy.

name: Acclaim Entertainment
summary: Aggressive licensing deals and mediocre movie tie-in games buried the publisher in debt until it liquidated in 2004.
lesson: Betting the business on movie-licensed games is a low-margin, high-risk strategy.

name: 3DO Company
summary: Trip Hawkins' console-and-publishing venture never recovered from the costly failure of its 3DO Interactive Multiplayer hardware, eventually filing for bankruptcy.
lesson: Launching proprietary game hardware against entrenched console makers requires far more capital than most startups can raise.

name: Trion Worlds
summary: The Rift and Defiance MMO studio never replicated early success at scale and was wound down, with remaining assets sold off piecemeal.
lesson: MMO development costs and live-ops overhead are brutal if a title doesn't retain a large enough subscriber base.

name: Machine Zone
summary: The Game of War/Mobile Strike maker built a massive ad-spend machine but couldn't sustain user acquisition costs as growth slowed, shedding staff and selling off assets.
lesson: Growth fueled primarily by expensive paid user acquisition is fragile once acquisition costs rise or engagement dips.

name: Google Stadia
summary: Google's cloud-gaming platform failed to attract enough users or publisher support and was shut down less than four years after launch.
lesson: A first-party platform needs a compelling exclusive library, not just impressive streaming technology, to win against entrenched incumbents.

name: OnLive
summary: A cloud-gaming pioneer years ahead of broadband infrastructure, OnLive burned through massive funding before selling its patents to Sony and shutting down.
lesson: Being technologically first doesn't matter if the underlying infrastructure isn't ready for mass adoption.

name: Mixer
summary: Microsoft's Twitch competitor signed exclusive deals with top streamers like Ninja but couldn't build critical mass and was shut down after four years.
lesson: Buying star creators can't manufacture the network effects a live-streaming platform needs organically.

name: Sky Mavis (Axie Infinity)
summary: The play-to-earn game's Ronin sidechain bridge was hacked for $625M in 2022, and player and economic activity collapsed as crypto gaming interest cooled.
lesson: A play-to-earn economy is only as strong as the security of its underlying blockchain infrastructure.

name: Arkane Austin
summary: Microsoft closed the Redfall developer after the live-service vampire shooter launched to poor reviews and low player counts, misjudging the studio's strengths.
lesson: Pushing a single-player-focused studio into an unfamiliar live-service genre can produce a launch failure that costs the studio its existence.

name: FaZe Clan
summary: The influencer-driven esports and content org went public via SPAC at a billion-dollar valuation, then saw its stock collapse over 90% amid heavy losses before going private.
lesson: A large creator and fan following doesn't automatically translate into a business with sustainable margins.

name: Loot Crate
summary: The gaming and geek-culture subscription box pioneer expanded too fast, mismanaged inventory and vendor payments, and filed for bankruptcy in 2019.
lesson: Subscription-box logistics and cash-flow management can sink a fast-growing consumer brand as easily as a lack of demand.

name: Improbable
summary: The SpatialOS cloud-simulation platform failed to gain broad developer adoption for large-scale multiplayer games, forcing a pivot to metaverse tooling and deep layoffs in 2023.
lesson: Infrastructure-layer bets on how games 'should' be built can stall if the developer ecosystem doesn't adopt the new paradigm.

name: Interplay Entertainment
summary: The Fallout/Baldur's Gate publisher spiraled into serious financial distress in the mid-2000s, selling off its most valuable IP (Fallout) to survive.
lesson: Selling your crown-jewel IP to stay solvent is often a company's last move before effective collapse.

name: Riot Games
summary: Built League of Legends into the world's most-played PC game via free-to-play plus cosmetic monetization and sustained long-term esports investment.
lesson: Free-to-play plus a long-term esports ecosystem investment can build durable network effects.

name: Supercell
funding: Bootstrapped then VC; majority sold to Tencent 2016 for $8.6B
summary: The Finnish studio's small autonomous 'cell' teams produced global mobile hits like Clash of Clans and Clash Royale with a tiny headcount.
lesson: Small, empowered teams with kill-fast discipline can outperform large studios on hit rate per dollar.

name: King Digital Entertainment
funding: IPO 2014, acquired by Activision Blizzard 2016 for $5.9B
summary: King turned Candy Crush Saga into one of mobile's longest-running hits through data-driven level design and freemium monetization.
lesson: Rigorous, ongoing level-by-level tuning based on player data can sustain a hit's revenue for a decade-plus.

name: Niantic
summary: Niantic's Pokémon Go became a global cultural phenomenon at launch by combining AR, real-world exploration, and a beloved existing IP.
lesson: Pairing new technology with a massive existing fan base can produce an outsized launch.

name: Epic Games
summary: Epic transformed from a shareware studio into a gaming juggernaut via Unreal Engine licensing and Fortnite's free-to-play battle royale success.
lesson: Owning both a hit consumer game and the underlying engine technology creates two reinforcing revenue streams.

name: Roblox
summary: Roblox built a user-generated-content gaming platform that let players create and monetize their own experiences, scaling to hundreds of millions of users.
lesson: Empowering users to create and monetize content themselves scales content supply far beyond what an internal studio could produce.

name: Mojang
summary: Markus Persson's Minecraft grew via word-of-mouth and modding community support into one of the best-selling games ever, self-funded before Microsoft's acquisition.
lesson: A simple, deeply moddable core mechanic can drive organic viral growth without heavy marketing spend.

name: Valve Corporation
summary: Valve built Steam into the dominant PC game distribution platform, turning a former games-only studio into a platform company with enormous margins.
lesson: Owning the distribution platform in a category can be more valuable than owning any single hit game.

name: Unity Technologies
summary: Unity grew from a niche Mac game engine into the most widely used game engine in the industry, powering the majority of mobile games.
lesson: Making powerful tools accessible and affordable to small and indie developers can build a massive long-term ecosystem moat.

name: Discord
summary: Built as a low-latency voice and text chat app for gamers, Discord became essential communication infrastructure for gaming communities and beyond.
lesson: Solving a genuine pain point for a passionate niche can expand into a much broader platform.

name: Twitch
summary: Twitch pivoted from general livestreaming to dedicated game streaming and became the dominant platform, leading to Amazon's acquisition.
lesson: A focused pivot into an underserved vertical can succeed where a broad platform struggled.

name: Rockstar Games
summary: Rockstar built the Grand Theft Auto and Red Dead Redemption franchises into some of entertainment's highest-grossing products through open-world innovation.
lesson: Sustained investment in open-world design innovation across a decade-plus can build franchises that dwarf typical game revenue.

name: Playrix
summary: The Karnauhov brothers built Playrix into one of the highest-grossing mobile game companies with Homescapes and Gardenscapes, entirely without outside venture funding.
lesson: A mobile game company can reach multi-billion-dollar revenue while remaining fully bootstrapped.

name: Scopely
funding: Acquired by Savvy Games Group 2023 for $4.9B
summary: Scopely scaled through licensed mobile hits like MONOPOLY GO! and Star Trek Fleet Command plus aggressive M&A, culminating in one of mobile gaming's largest acquisitions.
lesson: Combining strong licensed IP partnerships with disciplined live-ops can build durable, acquirable mobile revenue.

name: Bungie
summary: Bungie built Halo into a defining console franchise, spun out from Microsoft, then built Destiny into a live-service hit before Sony's acquisition.
lesson: A studio can successfully reinvent itself across multiple corporate ownership structures if its core creative talent stays intact.
